note:
勇敢的浏览器正在解决一个隐私问题，该问题将您访问的Torion URL地址泄漏到本地配置的DNS服务器，从而暴露了您访问的黑暗Web网站。
 Brave是基于Chromium的浏览器，在修改时会考虑到隐私，包括内置的广告拦截器，严格的数据控件以及用于匿名浏览Web的内置Tor浏览器模式。
 位于Tor上的网站使用用户只能通过Tor网络访问的洋葱URL地址。例如，DuckDuckGo的Tor地址为https://3g2upl4pq6kufc4m.onion/，而纽约时间的地址为https://www.nytimes3xbfgraghgh.onion/。
 要访问Torion网址，Brave添加了＆＃39;带Tor的私人窗户模式，作为Tor网络的代理。当您尝试连接到洋葱URL时，将通过志愿者运行的Tor节点代理您的请求，这些节点为您提出请求并发回返回的HTML。
  由于采用了这种代理方式，因此Brave的Tor模式不会直接提供与使用Tor浏览器相同的隐私级别。
  当使用Brave的Tor模式时，它应该将所有请求转发到Tor代理，并且不向任何非Tor Internet设备发送任何信息以增加隐私。
 但是，在带有Tor的Brave私人窗口中有一个bug；模式会导致您访问的任何Tor地址的洋葱URL也作为标准DNS查询发送到计算机配置的DNS服务器。 
此错误首先在Reddit帖子中报告，后来被PortSwigger研究总监James Kettle确认。
 BleepingComputer还通过使用Wireshark在使用Brave的Tor模式下查看DNS流量来验证了这些声明。
 如您在下面的视频中看到的那样，当您访问DuckDuckGo和NY Times＆＃39;洋葱URL在Brave的Tor浏览器模式下，浏览器还对我们本地配置的DNS服务器（IP地址为8.8.8.8的Google的公共服务器）执行DNS查询。
  Brave早在18天的GitHub项目页面上就已经报告了该错误，开发人员已经创建了一个修复程序。
 此问题是由勇敢的CNAME伪装广告阻止功能引起的，该功能阻止使用CNAME DNS记录模拟第一方脚本的第三方跟踪脚本。
 为了防止将Tor URL发送到已配置的DNS服务器，当处于Tor浏览模式时，Brave禁用了CNAME adblocking功能。
 ＆＃34;每次与@bridiver和@iefremov进行松弛讨论时，我们得出的结论是，现在为Tor禁用CNAME adblock是最好的选择。考虑到为了使DoH路由通过Tor，我们需要为dns事务删除LOAD_BYPASS_PROXY，但是当我们需要解析代理名称时，它可能会引入dns和代理代码循环。勇敢的开发人员在报告的问题中进行了解释。 
最初预期此修复程序将在Brave Browser Beta 1.21.x中推出，但Brave Browser开发人员Yan Zhu发推文称，此修补程序将提升至下一个稳定版本。  该文件原定于1.21.x发行（目前处于beta版），但鉴于它现在已公开，我们将提升到一个稳定的修补程序  — yan（@bcrypt）2021年2月19日
note_en:
Brave Browser is fixing a privacy issue that leaks the Tor onion URL addresses you visit to your locally configured DNS server, exposing the dark web websites you visit.
 Brave is Chromium-based browser that has been modified with privacy in mind, including a built-in ad blocker, tight data controls, and a built-in Tor browser mode to browse the web anonymously.
 Websites located on Tor use onion URL addresses that users can only access through the Tor network. For example, DuckDuckGo's Tor address is https://3g2upl4pq6kufc4m.onion/ and the New York Time's address is https://www.nytimes3xbfgragh.onion/.
 To access Tor onion URLs, Brave added a ' Private Window with Tor' mode that acts as a proxy to the Tor network. When you attempt to connect to an onion URL, your request is proxied through volunteer-run Tor nodes who make the request for you and send back the returned HTML.
  Due to this proxy implementation, Brave's Tor mode does not directly provide the same level of privacy as using the  Tor Browser.
  When using Brave's Tor mode, it should forward all requests to the Tor proxies and not send any information to any non-Tor Internet devices to increase privacy.
 However, a bug in Brave's 'Private window with Tor' mode is causing the onion URL for any Tor address you visit to also be sent as a standard DNS query to your machine's configured DNS server.
 This bug was first reported in a  Reddit post and later confirmed by  James Kettle, the Director of Research at PortSwigger.
 BleepingComputer has also verified the claims by using Wireshark to view DNS traffic while using Brave's Tor mode.
 As you can see in the video below, when visiting the DuckDuckGo and NY Times' onion URLs in Brave's Tor browser mode, the browser also performed DNS queries to our locally configured DNS server, Google's public servers at IP address 8.8.8.8.
  Brave is aware of this bug as it was  reported on their GitHub project page eighteen days ago, and developers have already created a fix.
 This issue is caused by Brave's CNAME decloaking ad-blocking feature that blocks third-party tracking scripts that  use CNAME DNS records to impersonate a first-party script.
 To prevent Tor URLs from being sent to configured DNS servers, Brave has disabled the CNAME adblocking feature when in the Tor browsing mode.
 "Per discussion on slack with  @bridiver and  @iefremov, we came to a conclusion that disabling CNAME adblock for Tor would be best option now. Considering in order to make DoH route through Tor, we need to remove  LOAD_BYPASS_PROXY for dns transaction but it might introduce dns and proxy code looping when we need to resolve proxy name," the Brave developers  explained in the reported issue.
 This fix was originally expected to roll out in the Brave Browser Beta 1.21.x but Brave Browser developer Yan Zhu tweeted that a hotfix will be uplifted to the next Stable version.
 this was scheduled to land in 1.21.x (currently in beta) but given that it's now public we will uplift to a stable hotfix
— yan (@bcrypt)  February 19, 2021
title: 勇敢的隐私错误将Tor Onion URL暴露给您的DNS提供商
title_en: Brave Privacy Bug Exposed Tor Onion URLs To Your DNS Provider

note:
总部位于马萨诸塞州的网络安全公司PiiQ Media进行的一项分析显示，主要社交媒体平台上的机器人一直在炒作GameStop和其他“模因”股票，这表明有组织的经济或外国参与者可能在Reddit驱动的交易狂潮中发挥了作用。
 在Reddit用户团结起来挤压对冲视频游戏零售商和其他公司的对冲基金之后，GameStop的股价在上个月飙升。 Reddit首席执行官史蒂夫·霍夫曼（Steve Huffman）本月告诉国会，具有自动内容的机器人，人工或虚假帐户在GameStop Reddit消息流量中并未发挥“重要作用”。
 PiiQ Media对Twitter，Facebook，Instagram和YouTube帖子的分析发现，尽管尚不清楚它们在整体传奇故事中的影响力，但机器人使用该平台推动GameStop和其他“ meme”股票。
 一家专注于社交媒体风险的初创公司，PiiQ表示，在1月28日狂潮到2月18日之前，他们在对话和个人资料中检查了诸如“ Hold the Line”和GameStop的股票代码“ GME”之类的关键词模式。 ，它还评估了一组不相关的股票上的职位。
  PiiQ表示，它在与GameStop相关的帖子中发现了非常相似的每日“开始和停止模式”，活动在交易日开始时开始，随后在交易日结束时出现大幅上涨。 PiiQ的联合创始人兼首席技术官Aaron Barr表示，这种模式表示机器人。
 “我们在其他四个社交媒体平台上看到了明显的人为行为模式。当您想到有机内容时，它每天都会变化，并且每天都会变化。一个月内每天都没有完全相同的模式，”他说。
 根据其真实性评分系统，PiiQ估计有成千上万的机器人帐户大肆炒作GameStop（模因股票）和Dogecoin（一种疯狂地风靡一时的加密货币）。它说，成千上万的假账户可以低至200美元购买。 
该公司没有分析Reddit数据，但Barr表示，他希望Reddit上会看到类似的活动模式，这表明类似机器人的对话或协调管理对话。
 Reddit的代表没有做出霍夫曼（Huffman）的证词。 YouTube，Facebook及其Instagram子公司的代表未回应置评请求。巴尔说，社交媒体平台通常会尝试清除有害的机器人。
  Twitter的一位发言人说，“机器人”已成为通俗易懂的用语，常常会误以为该帐户的性质。该公司指出，机器人可以在其平台上用于创意或创新目的。
 证券交易委员会（Securities and Exchange Commission）正在调查GameStop传奇事件，本月暂停了少数公司的交易，因为一些“社交媒体帐户可能正在采取协同行动来人为地影响其股票”。
 巴尔说，除交易员外，有组织犯罪分子还可能使用社交媒体来煽动资产价格，破坏美国市场的诚信是敌对国家行为者的一个已知目标。他说，但是目前尚不清楚这些影响力努力到底有多成功（如果有的话）。
note_en:
Bots on major social media platforms have been hyping up GameStop and other “meme” stocks, according to an analysis by Massachusetts-based cybersecurity company PiiQ Media, suggesting organized economic or foreign actors may have played a role in the Reddit-driven trading frenzy.
 Shares in GameStop  soared last month after Reddit users banded together to squeeze hedge funds that had bet against the video game retailer and other companies. Reddit Chief Executive Steve Huffman told Congress this month that bots, artificial or fake accounts with automated content, had not played a “significant role” in GameStop Reddit message traffic.
 PiiQ Media’s analysis of Twitter, Facebook, Instagram and YouTube posts, however, found that bots used the platforms  to push GameStop and other “meme” stocks, although it is unclear how influential they were in the overall saga.
 A startup that focuses on social media risks, PiiQ said it examined patterns of keywords such as “Hold the Line” and GameStop’s stock symbol, “GME” across conversations and profiles prior to the Jan. 28 frenzy, through Feb. 18. For comparison, it also assessed posts on an unrelated set of stocks.
  PiiQ said that it identified very similar daily “start and stop patterns” in the GameStop-related posts, with activity starting at the beginning of the trading day, followed by a large spike at the end of the trading day. Such patterns are indicative of bots, said Aaron Barr, co-founder and chief technology officer of PiiQ.
 “We saw clear patterns of artificial behavior across the other four social media platforms. When you think of organic content, it’s variable in the day, variable day-to-day. It doesn’t have the exact same pattern every day for a month,” he said.
 Based on its authenticity scoring system, PiiQ estimates there are tens of thousands of bot accounts hyping GameStop, the meme stocks, and Dogecoin, a cryptocurrency swept up in the frenzy. Thousands of fake accounts can be purchased for as little as $200, it said.
 The company did not analyze Reddit data, but Barr said he would expect to see a similar pattern of activity on Reddit, indicating bot-like or coordinated management of conversations.
 A representative for Reddit did not comment beyond Huffman’s testimony. Representatives for YouTube, Facebook and its Instagram subsidiary did not respond to requests for comment. The social media platforms generally try to weed out harmful bots, said Barr.
  A spokesperson for Twitter said that “bots” had become a catch-all term that can often mischaracterize the nature of the account. The company notes bots can be used on its platform for creative or innovative purposes.
 The Securities and Exchange Commission  is probing the GameStop saga, and this month suspended trading in a handful of companies because some “social media accounts may be engaged in a coordinated attempt to artificially influence” their stocks.
 In addition to traders, organized criminals may use social media to stoke asset prices, and undermining the integrity of U.S. markets is a known goal of hostile state actors, said Barr. But it is unclear how successful, if at all, these types of influence efforts are, he said.
title: 路透社

note:
尽管在过去的二十年中，警察一直在使用面部识别技术来试图在调查中识别陌生人，但使大多数美国人成为永久性照片阵容的做法却引起立法者和监管者的关注，这出乎意料地很少。到目前为止。
 由于担心隐私和准确性，立法者，公民自由倡导者和警察局长一直在辩论是否以及如何使用该技术。但是弄清楚如何规范它是棘手的。到目前为止，这意味着要么全有要么全无。奥克兰，波特兰，旧金山，明尼阿波利斯和其他地方的市议会已禁止警察使用该技术，这主要是因为该技术的工作方式偏颇。麻省理工学院研究人员和联邦政府最近几年的研究发现，许多面部识别算法对于白人来说最准确，而对其他所有人则不那么准确。
 同时，自动面部识别已成为一种强大的调查工具，有助于识别儿童mole亵者，在最近的一个备受瞩目的例子中，这些人还参与了参加1月6日在国会大厦暴动的人们。佛蒙特州的执法人员希望取消该州的禁令，因为“可能有数百名孩子等待被救。”
 这就是为什么马萨诸塞州的一项新法律如此有趣的原因：并非全部或全部。该州设法在调节技术上取得平衡，允许执法机构利用该工具的好处，同时建立保护措施，以防止以前发生的错误逮捕。
 一项将于7月生效的警察改革法案产生了新的护栏：警察首先必须获得法官的许可，然后才能进行面部识别搜索，然后由州警察局（F.B.I.）或汽车登记处执行搜索。本地官员不仅可以下载面部识别应用程序并进行搜索。
 该法律还设立了一个委员会来研究面部识别政策并提出建议，例如是否应告知刑事被告使用该技术对其进行了识别。
 如果您问州议员，他们是如何实现这一目标的，他们会经常把您推荐给一个人：马萨诸塞州ACLU的活动家Kade Crockford。 
“我担心的一个问题是，我们将在一个类似于菲利普·迪克（Philip K. Dick）小说《少数派报告》中描绘的世界中醒来的一天，无论走到哪里，都可以追踪到你的身体；您的身体运动，习惯，活动和位置都在一个可搜索的数据库中秘密地汇编和跟踪，可利用的数据库让上帝知道是谁，” Mx。克罗克福德说。
 两年前，即2019年6月，Mx。克罗克福德（Crockford）和马萨诸塞州的美国公民自由协会（ACLU）发起了一场反对面部监视的运动，向政策制定者介绍了该技术存在的问题，并通过公开记录要求调查了该技术在该州的使用范围。那个月，波士顿郊外的萨默维尔市通过了该州首次禁止使用政府的禁令。这是继上个月的旧金山之后，美国第二个这样做的城市。
 萨默维尔市议会议员本·埃文·坎彭说：“我们不想扮演“狂人”，并且禁止使用每一种新的功能异常的监视技术。” “我们想要一种选择参与的动力，如果社会决定他们想要的话，他们就能拥有它。”
  美国公民自由联盟（ACLU）向州和联邦机构提交了400多个公共记录请求，并发现警察经常使用该技术来识别人员，通常是通过将他们的脸庞放在州驾驶执照照片数据库中来进行。
 它收到的一条记录是马萨诸塞州州警官在2015年9月发送给所有“地方，州和联邦执法机构”的备忘录，提醒他们使用新的机动车登记系统电子邮件地址进行面部识别搜索。如果您不知道某人的身份，并想看看他们的脸是否与马萨诸塞州司机的脸相吻合，那么您所要做的就是通过电子邮件将照片发送到该地址。
 Mx说：“没有提及执法人员必须满足的任何政策或法律分析或法律门槛，才能进行这些搜索之一。”克罗克福德。
 当地警察机构发给ACLU的电子邮件还显示，在过去一年左右的时间里，许多警官都通过Clearview AI签署了试用帐户，该应用程序可以从公共网络上数十亿张照片中搜索某人的脸孔。 
到2020年，波士顿和马萨诸塞州的另外五个城市禁止政府使用面部识别。协助起草该州面部识别法案的民主党代表，州代表戴夫·罗杰斯（Dave Rogers）表示，城镇采取的举措有助于证明需要采取全州措施。众议员罗杰斯说：“我们看到执法部门以完全不受限制的方式使用它。” “我们社会中的技术正在比规范它的法律更快地发展。”
 民主党控制的立法机关通过的一项法案禁止几乎所有政府使用面部识别技术，但机动车登记处除外，后者使用该技术防止身份盗用。该部门只能使用搜查令对警察进行搜查。 （根据华盛顿州的一项法律，七月生效的逮捕令也是必需的。）
  贝克说：“我不会签署一项禁止面部识别的法案，”贝克说，该法案被用来解决两起凶杀案和对儿童的性虐待案件。
 尽管这只是较大的警察改革法案的一小部分，但面部识别准则却引起了人们的注意。 NBA球员Jaylen Brown和他的凯尔特人队友向《波士顿环球报》提交了一篇评论文章，谴责该技术的种族偏见问题并支持这项规定。
 他们写道：“尽管我们作为职业运动员的地位和形象，但我们也不免受到种族歧视和歧视性政策的影响。” “研究证实，人脸识别监控技术存在缺陷和偏见，在针对有色人种和女性使用时，错误率明显更高。”
 他们补充说：“我们不能允许有偏见的技术加强英联邦的种族主义政策。”
 最终，立法者和州长以悬而未决的法规形式达成妥协。 
包括ACLU其他办公室在内的一些批评家说，面部识别具有独特的危害性，必须予以禁止。警察工会和波士顿警察局未回应置评请求。马萨诸塞州斯普林菲尔德市警察局的新闻官员瑞安·沃尔什（Ryan Walsh）表示，该部门并不认为这项措施是执法部门如何使用该技术的最后手段。
 他说：“尽管我们目前不使用或没有计划使用任何面部识别软件，但我们希望法律随着技术的发展和改进而发展。”
 Mx。克罗克福德（Crockford）自2009年加入马萨诸塞州美国公民自由协会（ACLU）以来一直致力于技术和监视问题，他说，禁止在该州使用面部识别在“政治上是不可能的”。但她认为，其他准则将有助于防止滥用和误捕。
 罗杰斯先生和州参议员辛西娅·克里姆（Cynthia Creem）提出了一项新法案，但有限制条件，包括限制在公共场所使用该技术。
note_en:
Though police have been using facial recognition technology for the  last two decades to try to identify unknown people in their investigations, the practice of putting the majority of Americans into a  perpetual photo lineup has gotten surprisingly little attention from lawmakers and regulators. Until now.
 Lawmakers, civil liberties advocates and police chiefs have debated whether and how to use the technology because of concerns about both privacy and accuracy. But figuring out how to regulate it is tricky. So far, that has meant an all-or-nothing approach. City Councils in Oakland, Portland,  San Francisco,  Minneapolis and elsewhere have banned police use of the technology, largely because of bias in how it works. Studies in recent years by MIT researchers and the federal government found that many facial recognition algorithms are most accurate for white men, but less so for everyone else.
 At the same time, automated facial recognition has become a powerful investigative tool, helping to identify  child molesters and, in a recent high-profile example,  people who participated in the Jan. 6 riot at the Capitol. Law enforcement officials in Vermont want the state’s ban lifted because there “could be hundreds of kids waiting to be saved.”
 That’s why a new law in Massachusetts is so interesting: It’s not all or nothing. The state managed to strike a balance on regulating the technology, allowing law enforcement to harness the benefits of the tool, while building in protections that might prevent the  false arrests that have happened  before.
 A police reform bill that goes into effect in July creates new guardrails: Police first must get a judge’s permission before running a face recognition search, and then have someone from the state police, the F.B.I. or the Registry of Motor Vehicles perform the search. A local officer can’t just download a facial recognition app and do a search.
 The law also creates a commission to study facial recognition policies and make recommendations, such as whether a criminal defendant should be told that they were identified using the technology.
 If you ask lawmakers in the state how they pulled it off, they will frequently refer you to one person: Kade Crockford, an activist at the ACLU of Massachusetts.
 “One of my concerns was that we would wake up one day in a world resembling that depicted in the Philip K. Dick novel “Minority Report,” where everywhere you go, your body is tracked; your physical movements, habits, activities and locations are secretly compiled and tracked in a searchable database available to god knows who,” Mx. Crockford said.
 Two years ago, in June 2019, Mx. Crockford and the ACLU of Massachusetts launched a campaign against face surveillance, educating policymakers about problems with the technology and investigating, via public records requests, how widely it was used in the state.  That month, the city of Somerville, outside Boston,  passed the state’s first ban on government use. It was the second city in the country to do so, after San Francisco the month before.
 “We don’t want to play Whac-a-Mole and ban every new dystopic piece of surveillance technology,” said Ben Ewen-Campen, a member of the Somerville City Council. “We want an opt-in dynamic where if society decides they want it, they can have it.”
  The ACLU submitted over 400  public record requests to state and federal agencies and found that police regularly used the technology to identify people, usually by running their faces against the state database of driver’s license photos.
 One record it received was  a September 2015 memo sent by a Massachusetts state police officer to all “local, state and federal law enforcement agencies,” alerting them to a new Registry of Motor Vehicles email address for facial recognition searches. If you didn’t know someone’s identity and wanted to see if their face matched that of a Massachusetts driver, all you had to do was email a photo to that address.
 “There was no mention of any policy or legal analysis or legal threshold that law enforcement would have to meet for one of these searches to be performed,” said Mx. Crockford.
 Emails from local police agencies turned over to the ACLU also revealed that over the last year or so, a number of officers signed up for trial accounts with Clearview AI, an app that searches for someone’s face from billions of photos on the public web.
 By 2020, Boston and five more cities in Massachusetts banned government use of facial recognition. State representative Dave Rogers, a Democrat who helped to craft the state’s facial recognition bill, said the initiatives by cities and towns helped demonstrate the need for a statewide measure. “We saw that law enforcement was using it in a completely unfettered way,” Rep. Rogers said. “Technology in our society is advancing much more rapidly than the law that regulates it.”
 A bill passed by the Democrat-controlled legislature banned almost all government use of facial recognition technology, except for the Registry of Motor Vehicles, which uses it to prevent identity theft. The department could run searches for police only with a search warrant. (A warrant is required under a  Washington state law that also takes effect in July.)
  “I’m not going to sign a bill into law that bans facial recognition,” Mr. Baker said, according to  a local report, citing its use in solving two cases of homicide and child sexual abuse.
 Though it was a small part of a larger police reform bill, the facial recognition guidelines attracted attention. NBA player Jaylen Brown and his Celtics teammates submitted  an opinion article to the Boston Globe decrying the technology’s racial bias problems and supporting the regulation.
 “Despite our positions and profiles as professional athletes, we are not immune to racial profiling and discriminatory policing,” they wrote. “Studies confirm that face recognition surveillance technology is  flawed and biased, with significantly higher error rates when used against people of color and women.”
 “We can’t allow biased technology to supercharge racist policing in the Commonwealth,” they added.
 Eventually the legislators and the governor reached a compromise, in the form of the pending regulations.
 Some critics, including other ACLU offices, say that facial recognition is uniquely harmful and  must be banned. Police unions and the Boston Police Department did not respond to requests for comment. Ryan Walsh, a public information officer with the Springfield, Mass., police department, indicated that the department does not see this measure as the last word on how law enforcement can use this technology.
 “While we do not currently use or have plans to use any facial recognition software, we hope the law evolves as the technology evolves and improves,” he said.
 Mx. Crockford, who has been working on technology and surveillance issues since joining the ACLU of Massachusetts in 2009, said that it was “politically impossible” to ban the use of facial recognition in the state. But she believes that additional guidelines will help prevent abuse and false arrests.
 Mr. Rogers and state senator Cynthia Creem have introduced  a new bill with restrictions that include curbs on the use of the technology in public places.
title: ACLU和活动家Kade Crockford如何说服马萨诸塞州立法者通过一项警察改革法案，禁止在刑事案件中使用面部识别技术
title_en: How ACLU and activist Kade Crockford convinced Massachusetts lawmakers to pass a police reform bill banning the use of facial recognition tech in criminal cases

note:
这是Facebook上的广告，面膜翻倍，使发箍变硬，这使Dan Castle感到绝望。
 他的公司CastleGrade制造了一种可重复使用的高过滤面罩，在牙医，教师和与他人相邻的工作人员中很流行，并且愿意支付$ 44.99。
 但是自从八月份以来，他一直无法在Facebook上出售自己的商品，当时该公司突然封锁了他的广告，理由是旨在确保为医护人员保留医用口罩的政策。他说，从那以后，每天的销售额从40,000美元下降到5,000美元。然而，他始终都看到非医用级口罩的广告。
 他说：“这些公司拥有如此大的垄断地位，如果没有它们，您真的无法开展业务。” “政策根本没有道理。”
 卡斯尔先生在Facebook上的经历与其他最近开始生产N95和其他医用级口罩的其他小型口罩生产商的情况相呼应。他们主要是被医院网络拒之门外，他们希望在网上出售高渗透率产品，而美国人会在这里购物。但是，即使科学家敦促人们将他们的面部覆盖物升级为可以阻挡引起感染的微小病原体的面膜，科技巨头也并没有因此而变得容易。
 Google和Facebook禁止销售医用级口罩，而Amazon则将其限制在购物者手中，这是在大流行初期几个月制定的政策，当时医院正争先恐后地购买防护装备。
 但是一些公共卫生专家和口罩制造商表示，这些规定已经过时，特别是考虑到更多的传染性冠状病毒变种的传播以及大量自制的口罩正在全国各地的仓库中集尘。他们说，这些限制可能会阻碍该国在疫苗普及之前的几个月内限制新感染的能力。 
布莱根妇女医院的研究人员Abraar Karan博士说：“即使现在情况有所下降，我们仍需要人们戴上高过滤口罩，以防止任何形式的超级扩散死灰复燃，尤其是在使用这些新变体的情况下。”哈佛医学院一直在推动一项旨在资助和向公众分发高过滤口罩的国家计划。
  电子商务平台表示，他们正在从疾病控制和预防中心获取线索，该中心继续建议，在全国短缺的情况下，优先将N95s分配给医务人员。
 但是像3M和霍尼韦尔这样制造口罩的庞然大物已经大大增加了生产，以满足医务人员的需求，而在大流行初期几个月就突然中断了出口的中国，又一次以便宜的N95淹没了美国。
 “我很乐意将口罩卖给医护人员，但眼下医院还没敲门。”位于新泽西州帕特森的成立了一年的公司Protective Health Gear首席执行官布莱恩·沃林（Brian Wolin）说。 ，在其工厂中有50万枚未售出的N95口罩。
 Facebook和Instagram禁止在医疗级口罩上做广告，但允许使用大量分类为非医疗级的防护装备，包括宽松的塑料面罩和用日本纺织品制成的面罩，可作为奢侈品消费者的腰包。卡斯尔说，这种产品似乎违反了冠状病毒感染控制的基本概念。
 他说：“这令人沮丧，因为我们遵守规则，无法休息。” “但这尤其令人不安，因为在这种情况下，生命受到威胁。”
 拥有Instagram的Facebook在一份声明中表示，其禁止销售医用口罩的规定是基于联邦卫生当局的指导，以及必须控制销售假冒口罩的需要。该公司表示：``我们会定期评估政策，同时平衡工作以帮助中小型品牌发展业务。'' 
许多面具初创企业尤其对亚马逊感到沮丧。与Facebook和Google实施的全面禁令不同，亚马逊允许在其网站上出售各种各样的口罩，但亚马逊的算法与看似矛盾的政策相结合，对买卖双方来说都是烦人的。
 假冒面具在平台上仍然是一个问题，尽管亚马逊表示正在努力淘汰假冒版本。
 对于任何试图在其网站上购买N95口罩的人来说，这种困惑都是显而易见的。
 亚马逊允许出售N95，但实际上已垄断了金佰利，克拉克，Halyard Health和Makrite等少数几家大公司的产品，该公司在该网站上批量购买其产品，然后直接从亚马逊仓库运送给购物者。除非购买者拥有亚马逊商业账户，否则出售N95的小公司通常无法直接将其产品出售给消费者。
 搜索N95口罩的个人客户会收到KN95，这是一种比N95效果差的中文口罩。亚马逊的产品可能会每天变化。在很多时候，普通的购物者都会收到一条消息，说该商品不可用，因为“对于响应Covid-19的前线组织优先。”
 亚马逊表示正在努力招募新的个人防护设备或P.P.E.供应商。该公司在一封电子邮件中说：“我们的团队不断监控P.P.E.的供应，包括N95口罩，以确保我们能够满足医疗保健客户以及一般企业和家庭客户的需求。”
 加利福尼亚制造商T3 Gear的首席执行官迈克尔·阿特金森（Michael Atkinson）在销售其公司去年开始生产的高过滤面罩时，无法弄清楚亚马逊-其政策或算法。 
首先，亚马逊要求查看测试数据，以证明他的口罩至少过滤掉了造成冠状病毒的微小病原体的95％。至少，这使他获得了在亚马逊网站上出售产品的批准。
  但是从那以后的几个月中，亚马逊有时阻止顾客购买口罩。 Atkinson先生说，即使在允许销售的情况下，该平台的算法也有效地将他的产品列表掩埋在几十个KN95之下。
 阿特金森说：“亚马逊一直在不加解释的情况下更改规则。” “我意识到他们不是慈善机构，但是像我们这样的小公司却没有机会。”
 亚马逊前高管，市场咨询公司Buy Box Experts的首席战略官詹姆斯·汤姆森（James Thomson）表示，在线零售巨头与小型口罩制造商之间的紧张关系反​​映了对主导零售领域的在线平台的巨大力量的更大争议。汤姆森的公司帮助品牌商应对亚马逊复杂的销售政策。他说，其看似矛盾的N95口罩方法（声称此类产品专供医务人员使用，但允许批量购买的口罩除外），可能是亚马逊的基石。维持消费者忠诚度的策略。
 汤姆森先生说：“即使他们在那个面具上几乎赚不到钱，也实际上是要让客户满意，这样他们才不会去别的地方。” “问题是，如果他们允许这些做法扩大规模，就会对除亚马逊以外的其他任何事物造成破坏。”
 很难夸大科技巨头的销售能力。明尼苏达州初创公司Breathe99的联合创始人马克斯·博克-阿龙森（Max Bock-Aronson）说，自从Facebook去年12月投放广告以来，可洗式面膜过滤掉了99.6％的微观颗粒，他的公司一直处于困境，下降了50％在打折。博克·阿隆森先生说：“由于我们的现金流紧缩，我们只能制造小批量的口罩，但它们会立即售罄。”
 他说，由于Facebook不愿处理围绕其平台上与政治和大流行相关的内容的错误信息，该公司声称需要保护公众感到特别生气。 
他说：“这令人沮丧，因为我们挥舞着双手，说‘嘿，我们有更好的面具可以保护人们，”但是我们真的不允许在他们的网站上谈论它。 “平时很难开展业务，但是当这些公司将您拒之门外时，几乎是不可能的。”  Facebook，谷歌和亚马逊在声明中表示，他们没有立即计划修改其政策。
note_en:
It was the Facebook ad for a mask doubling as a hair scrunchie that pushed Dan Castle to despair.
 His company,  CastleGrade, makes a reusable, high-filtration face mask that has been popular among dentists, teachers and those who work in proximity to others — and willing to pay $44.99.
 But he has been unable to sell his wares on Facebook since August, when the company abruptly blocked his ads, citing a policy aimed at ensuring medical-grade masks are reserved for health care workers. Since then, he said, sales have plummeted to $5,000 a day from $40,000. And yet, he sees ads for nonmedical grade masks all of the time.
 “These companies have such a monopoly that you really can’t be in business without them,” he said. “The policy just doesn’t make sense.”
 Mr. Castle’s experiences with Facebook echoed those of other small mask producers who have recently began making N95s and other medical grade masks. Largely shut out by hospital networks, they had hoped to sell their high-filtration products online, where Americans do much of their shopping. But the tech giants have not made it easy, even as scientists have urged people to upgrade their face coverings to those that can block the tiny pathogens that cause infection.
 Google and Facebook ban the sale of medical-grade masks, and Amazon limits their availability to shoppers — policies born during the early months of the pandemic, when hospitals were scrambling to obtain protective gear.
 But some public health experts and mask manufacturers say these rules are outdated, especially given the spread of more infectious coronavirus variants and the abundance of domestically made masks that are gathering dust in warehouses across the country. The restrictions, they say, may hinder the country’s ability to limit new infections in the months before vaccinations become more widely available.
 “Even though cases are coming down right now, we need people to be wearing high-filtration masks to prevent any sort of super spreading resurgences, particularly with these new variants,” said Dr. Abraar Karan, a researcher at Brigham and Women’s Hospital and Harvard Medical School who has been  pushing for a national program to subsidize and distribute high-filtration masks to the public.
  The e-commerce platforms say they are taking their cues from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention,  which continues to recommend that N95s be prioritized for medical personnel amid a national shortage.
 But mask-making behemoths like 3M and Honeywell have drastically increased production to meet the needs of medical workers, and China, which abruptly cut off exports in the early months of the pandemic, is once again flooding the United States with cheaper N95s.
 “I’d be happy to sell my masks to health care workers, but right now hospitals aren’t exactly banging down my door,” said Brian Wolin, the chief executive of  Protective Health Gear, a year-old company in Paterson, N.J., that has a half million unsold N95 masks at its factory.
 Facebook and Instagram prohibit advertisements for medical-grade masks but allow a large array of protective gear categorized as nonmedical grade, including loosefitting  plastic face shields and face coverings made from Japanese textiles that double as  pocket squares for the luxury consumer. Such products, Mr. Castle said, would seem to violate basic notions of coronavirus infection control.
 “It’s disheartening because we are playing by the rules and we can’t catch a break,” he said. “But it’s especially upsetting because, in this case, lives are at stake.”
 In a statement, Facebook, which owns Instagram, said its prohibitions on the sale of medical masks were based on guidance from federal health authorities and the need to rein in the sale counterfeit masks. “We regularly evaluate our policies​ while balancing our work to help small and midsize brands grow their business,” the company said.
 Many mask start-ups have been especially frustrated with Amazon. Unlike the blanket bans imposed by Facebook and Google, Amazon allows a wide range of masks to be sold on its site, but the company’s algorithms combined with its seemingly contradictory policies can prove vexing for both buyers and sellers.
 Counterfeit masks also remain a problem on the platform, though Amazon said it was working to weed out fake versions.
 The confusion is apparent to anyone who has tried buying an N95 mask on its site.
 Amazon allows the sale of N95s, but it has effectively given a monopoly to a handful of big companies like Kimberly-Clark, Halyard Health and Makrite, whose products the site buys in bulk and then ships to shoppers directly from Amazon warehouses. Small companies that sell N95s are often blocked from selling their wares directly to consumers unless the buyer has an Amazon business account.
 Individual customers who search for N95 masks receive a welter of KN95s, a Chinese mask that is less effective than the N95s. Amazon’s offerings can change from day to day. Much of the time, ordinary shoppers are greeted with a message that says the item is unavailable because it is “prioritized for organizations on the front lines responding to Covid-19.”
 Amazon says it is working to bring on new vendors of personal protective equipment, or P.P.E. “Our teams continually monitor the supply of P.P.E., including N95 masks, to ensure we can meet the demand for health care customer needs, as well as general business and at-home customers,” the company said in an email.
 Michael Atkinson, the chief executive of  T3 Gear, a California manufacturer, can’t figure out Amazon — its policies or its algorithms — when it comes to selling the high-filtration face masks that his company began making last year.
 First, Amazon demanded to see the testing data to prove his claims that the masks filter out at least 95 percent of the tiny pathogens that cause the coronavirus. That, at least, won him approval to sell on Amazon’s website.
  But in the months since then, Amazon has at times stopped customers from buying the masks. Even when sales are allowed, Mr. Atkinson said the platform’s algorithms effectively buried his product listing beneath scores of KN95s.
 “Amazon changes the rules all the time without explanation,” Mr. Atkinson said. “I realize they’re not a charity, but a small company like ours doesn’t stand a chance.”
 James Thomson, a former Amazon executive who is the chief strategy officer at the marketing consultancy  Buy Box Experts, said the tensions between online retailing giants and small mask-makers reflected the larger debate over the outsize power of the online platforms that dominated the retailing landscape. Mr. Thomson, whose company helps brands navigate Amazon’s complex sales policies, said its seemingly contradictory approach to N95 masks — claiming such wares are reserved for medical personnel but then allowing exceptions for masks they have purchased in bulk — is probably an outgrowth of Amazon’s bedrock strategy for maintaining consumer loyalty.
 “Even if they make next to no money on that mask, it’s really about keeping customers happy so they don’t go somewhere else,” Mr. Thomson said. “The problem is, if they allow these practices to scale up, it becomes disruptive to anything else that isn’t Amazon.”
 It is hard to overstate the selling power of the tech giants. Max Bock-Aronson, a co-founder of  Breathe99, a start-up in Minnesota whose washable face mask filters out 99.6 percent of microscopic particles, said his company had been ailing since Facebook dropped its ads in December, leading to a 50 percent drop in sales. “Because of our cash flow crunch, we can only make small batches of masks but they sell out immediately,” Mr. Bock-Aronson said.
 He says he is especially irked by the company’s claims about needing to protect the public given Facebook’s reluctance to tackle misinformation surrounding political and pandemic-related content on its platforms.
 “It’s just frustrating because we’re waving our hands, saying ‘Hey, we have a better mask that can protect people,’ but we’re really not allowed to talk about it on their site,” he said. “It’s hard enough to start a business in normal times, but it’s next to impossible when these companies shut you out of the marketplace.”
 In statements, Facebook, Google and Amazon said they had no immediate plans to revise their policies.
title: 批评人士说，从大流行初期开始，亚马逊，谷歌和Facebook限制医用级口罩销售的政策现在已经过时。
title_en: Critics say the policies of Amazon, Google, and Facebook from the early days of the pandemic to restrict the sale of medical-grade masks are now outdated

note:
随着Covid-19疫苗接种计划的推出，美国面临着历史上最大的后勤挑战之一，许多美国人问为什么大科技公司不能开展这项行动。如果亚马逊可以在两天内交付电话充电器，为什么它不能利用其后勤能力迅速为一个国家接种疫苗？利用Google收集的有关其用户的所有数据，为什么它无法追踪并确定谁需要接种疫苗？
 这主要是因为在交付手机充电器时，像亚马逊这样的公司处理从订购到交付的整个过程。但是，在疫苗分配方面，科技公司已经知道他们的控制权要少得多。联邦政府正在为所需的药物付款，并正在管理向各州的药品供应。然后，各州，县和公司接管并弄清楚如何将疫苗带入武器。因此，尽管“大技术”肯定在疫苗推出中发挥了作用，但它并不完全是人们所期望的。
   J.T.说：“我们有这种大流行的规模：您正在为3亿多人接种疫苗。”莱恩（Lane），是国家和地区卫生官员协会的创新官员，这是一个公共卫生官员的专业组织。 “这是我们一生中最大的努力。”
  以爱荷华州最近发生的情况为例：该州仅用了一周的时间便取消了与微软的合同，以运行其疫苗预订系统。但是官员们说，这不是因为微软的技术不足。即使对于像Microsoft这样的科技巨头来说，也很难将99个县中现有数字基础设施的拼凑而成。
 微软发言人蒙塔纳·麦克拉克兰（Montana MacLachlan）表示：“我们了解并支持爱荷华州决定优化其现有系统，而不是在其许多现有平台上构建不同的系统。”
  正如爱荷华州州长金·雷诺兹（Kow Reynolds）在2月17日的新闻发布会上所说的那样：“如果不对他们当前的系统造成重大破坏，就不可能及时实现，而且我们也不想放慢进度。”
   科技公司肯定从一开始就尝试帮助应对大流行病。大约一年前，Google和Apple联手帮助进行联系人跟踪。但是大多数州最终都不接受该技术，并且几乎没有实质性影响。疫苗的推出终于包含了很久以前开发的某些Big Tech技术：疾病控制和预防中心鼓励该国使用的VaccineFinder网站由Google创立，并于2012年由波士顿儿童医院接管。 
其他科技公司正在以完全不同的方式在疫苗推出中占据一席之地。 Uber和Lyft提供了往返于疫苗预约的免费接送服务。 Google和Verizon都在为宣传疫苗意识的广告付费。谷歌和苹果已经参加了有关可能的疫苗护照的讨论，但尚未宣布任何具体计划。
 像亚马逊这样的公司正通过直接与白宫合作来规避整个本地疫苗的推出。在就职典礼当天，亚马逊高管向拜登政府提供了分发疫苗的帮助，并强调该公司自己的80万名美国工人应该是首批获得这种疫苗的人之一。亚马逊和白宫未经公开讲话的消息来源说，拜登的团队目前正在与亚马逊进行谈判，以探讨该公司如何协助总统的国家疫苗接种计划。
   但是，像Google，Salesforce和Microsoft这样的大型高科技公司提供帮助的最常见方式之一就是取得好坏参半的结果。那是因为他们正在尝试创建全州范围的网站，以便人们安排疫苗接种约会。
  2月10日，新泽西州州长菲尔·墨菲（Phil Murphy）表示，微软网站的推出为该州带来了“真正的挑战”。一位无权在记录上发言的墨菲（Murphy）行政高级官员称，微软为帮助新泽西州所做的努力是“低于标准的体验”。她说，微软的平台“配置错误”，直到本月才经常崩溃。甚至在某些情况下，寻求疫苗的人也可以在不活跃或关闭的地点注册约会。
 她说：“当科技公司举手时，我们作为国家和作为公众代表都有权获得与销售毛衣的公司一样的优质服务和正常运行时间，我们不应有不合标准的经历，” 。
 微软发言人麦克拉克兰（MacLachlan）在一封电子邮件中说，该公司正在与新泽西合作，“以尽可能快，安全和有效的方式提供疫苗接种，其中包括解决一些技术问题。”
 包括加利福尼亚在内的其他州也试图建立共享门户网站以进行疫苗接种签约，但这些站点的人口有限。加利福尼亚的统一站点MyTurn由Salesforce和一个名为Skedulo的湾区初创公司提供支持。 
但目前，由加利福尼亚州公共卫生部管理的MyTurn仅向该州大部分城市提供疫苗，并已将许多郊区县以及一些农业和农村地区排除在外。该州范围内的在线搜索工具并未考虑CVS和Walgreens这两家大型零售商，它们拥有完全独立的疫苗渠道，并且没有寻求硅谷的帮助。它还没有考虑该州主要的医疗保健提供者，例如Kaiser Permanente，它们正在运行各自的注册系统。
 但是公共卫生专家说，这些挑战并不一定是Big Tech的错。科技公司陷入了州和县卫生部门如何设置疫苗签约的困境。一些系统着重于为尽可能多的人接种疫苗。但是其他公共卫生部门的重点是为那些无法获得可靠互联网访问的被忽视的老年人，少数民族和低收入人群接种疫苗。
 每个人都在谈论这种疫苗的公平性。但是，如果有选择的话，大多数人会更喜欢使用量，因为它可以使我们更快地摆脱大流行。”旧金山加利福尼亚大学流行病学和生物统计学教授金·罗德斯博士说，他最近的研究对Covid-19进行了研究。湾区黑人社区的感染率。 “那是关于网站崩溃和这些技术系统不堪重负的事情。我们要在那里增加销量。”
   尽管如此，一些大型科技公司仍在与地方政府打交道。预计Salesforce将为内华达州即将发布的网站提供引擎。根据Salesforce发言人Maha Neouchy的说法，该公司正为“超过50个联邦，州，地方和私人卫生组织”提供技术服务，以进行疫苗管理。其中还包括伊利诺伊州莱克县。她拒绝评论费用。微软还与俄克拉荷马州，蒙大拿州和威斯康星州签署了合同，以帮助提供各州的在线疫苗接种计划门户。
  地方政府似乎也愿意为这些计划付费。公开记录要求显示，从去年12月到7月底，俄克拉荷马州已向微软支付了近50万美元，以运行其系统。这几乎占俄克拉荷马州卫生部5900万美元信息技术预算的1％。
 同样，芝加哥北部湖县的其他公共记录显示，Salesforce的合作伙伴公司Carahsoft告诉该县，从10月下旬开始交付一项名为AllVax的“疫苗管理解决方案”将花费550万美元。到2020年底。该县从2021年初开始接管该数字系统的运营。
 某些当地司法管辖区对大型高科技公司如何帮助他们迎接数量可观的令人生畏的网站访问者感到满意。 
“总的来说，我为该系统感到非常自豪，而且我们还没有发生过故障-我们已经在该系统中注册了380,000人，”卫生信息学和技术总监Jefferson McMillan-Wilhoit说。为湖县卫生厅。他指出，如果没有与Salesforce达成的550万美元的交易（这是其部门8000万美元的年度预算的重要组成部分），那么这么快地建立这种数字基础设施几乎是不可能的。
 北卡罗莱纳州最近与Google签署了一项近110万美元的合同，为明年提供服务，以创建一个统一的州级系统，而不是让公民和县级公共卫生实体经营自己的系统。
 Sam Gibbs说：“我们将它站起来并投入使用，并在四个小时的时间内对140万个用户进行了压力测试，并在同一个时间段内获得了成千上万的点击量。”北卡罗来纳州技术和运营部副部长指出，该州的人口约为1050万。
   但是公共卫生官员指出，无论高科技公司如何创建疫苗接种注册网站，都有一些他们永远无法到达的关键人群。那是因为仍然有数百万美国人需要疫苗，但无法上网甚至无法注册。 Rhoads建议，地方卫生部门应设法与那些没有技能，互联网访问权限或不信任大型高科技公司使用这些站点的人群建立联系。
  “更大不是更好。而且它只是回到数量与股权的关系上，” Rhoads说。 “如果我们做大，我们将不会获得公平。考虑到这一点，如果我们将交易量作为优先事项，那么当我们没有获得公平的回报时，我们不要感到惊讶。”
 赛勒斯·法里瓦（Cyrus Farivar）是位于旧金山的NBC News的技术调查部门的记者。
 April Glaser是旧金山NBC新闻技术调查小组的记者。
note_en:
As the nation takes on one of the largest logistical challenges in history with the rollout of the Covid-19 vaccination program, many Americans have asked why Big Tech can't run the operation. If Amazon can deliver a phone charger in two days, why can’t it use its logistical prowess to speedily vaccinate a nation? With all of the data that Google collects about its users, why can’t it track down and identify who needs a vaccination?
 That’s largely because when it comes to getting a phone charger delivered, a company like Amazon handles every step of the process, from taking the order to delivery. But when it comes to vaccine distribution, tech companies have learned they have far less control. The  federal government is paying for the needed medication and is  managing the supply to the states. Then, states, counties and corporations take over and figure out how to get vaccines into arms. So while Big Tech is certainly playing a role in the vaccine rollout, it’s not exactly how people may expect.
   “We have the scale of this pandemic: you’re trying to vaccinate 300 million-plus people,” said J.T. Lane, the innovation officer for the Association of State and Territorial Health Officials, a professional organization for public health officials. “That’s the biggest effort of our lifetime.”
  Take for example what recently happened in Iowa: It took just one week for the state to cancel its contract with Microsoft to run its vaccination booking system. But officials said it was not because Microsoft’s technology was insufficient. It was too difficult even for a tech giant like Microsoft to combine the patchwork of existing digital infrastructure across 99 counties.
 “We understand and support the state of Iowa’s decision to optimize their existing system rather than building a different system across many of their existing platforms,” said Montana MacLachlan, a Microsoft spokesperson.
  As Iowa Gov. Kim Reynolds said  at a Feb. 17 news conference, “It would not be possible in a timely manner without significant disruption to their current systems, and we did not want to slow down the progress that we’re making.”
   Tech companies have certainly tried from the start to help with the pandemic response. Nearly a year ago, Google and Apple joined forces to help with contact tracing. But most states ultimately didn’t embrace the technology and it had little  substantive impact. Some of Big Tech’s technology that was developed long ago is finally being embraced in the vaccine rollout: the VaccineFinder website that the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention is encouraging the nation to use was started by Google and taken over by Boston Children’s Hospital in 2012.
 Other tech companies are carving out their place in the vaccine rollout in entirely different ways.  Uber and Lyft have offered free rides to and from vaccination appointments.  Google and  Verizon are helping pay for advertisements promoting vaccine awareness. Google and Apple have  participated in discussions surrounding possible vaccine passports, but have not announced any concrete plans.
 Companies like Amazon are circumventing the entire local vaccine rollout by working directly with the White House. On Inauguration Day, Amazon executives offered the Biden administration help in distributing the vaccines, stressing that the company’s own 800,000 U.S. workers should be among the first in line to get it. Biden’s team is  currently in talks with Amazon about how the company can assist with the president’s national vaccination program, according to sources at Amazon and the White House who are not authorized to speak publicly.
   But one of the most common ways Big Tech companies like Google, Salesforce and Microsoft are helping is meeting mixed results. That’s because they’re trying to create statewide websites that let people schedule vaccination appointments.
  On Feb. 10, New Jersey  Gov. Phil Murphy said that Microsoft’s website rollout presented “real challenges” for the state. A senior Murphy administration official who was not authorized to speak on the record described Microsoft’s efforts to help New Jersey as a “subpar experience.” She said that Microsoft’s platform was “misconfigured” and was regularly crashing as recently as this month. There were even instances where vaccine-seekers could sign up for appointments at sites that were inactive or closed.
 “When tech companies put up their hands — we as the state and we as representatives of the public are entitled to as good of a service and uptime as a company selling sweaters, and we shouldn’t be having a substandard experience,” she said.
 MacLachlan, the Microsoft spokesperson, said in an email that the company was working with New Jersey “to deliver vaccinations as quickly, safely and efficiently as possible, and that includes addressing some technical issues.”
 Other states, including California, have also attempted to set up a shared portal for vaccination signups, but those sites reach limited populations. California’s unified site, called  MyTurn, is powered by Salesforce and a Bay Area startup called Skedulo.
  But for now, MyTurn, which is run by the California Department of Public Health, only offers vaccines to much of the state’s urban area and has left out many suburban counties along with some agricultural and rural areas. That statewide online search tool does not account for CVS and Walgreens, two large retailers that have entirely separate pipelines of vaccines and are not enlisting Silicon Valley for help. It also does not account for major health care providers across the state, like Kaiser Permanente, which are running their own separate signup systems.
 But public health experts say that these challenges are not necessarily Big Tech’s fault. Tech companies are caught between how state and county health departments set up vaccination sign-ups. Some systems focus on vaccinating as many people as possible. But other public health departments are focused on vaccinating overlooked elderly, minority and low-income populations who have less reliable internet access.
 “Everyone is talking about equity with the vaccine. But given a choice, most people would prefer volume because it would get us out of the pandemic faster,” said  Dr. Kim Rhoads, a professor of epidemiology and biostatistics at the University of California, San Francisco, whose  recent research examined Covid-19 infection rates in Black communities in the Bay Area. “That’s the thing about website crashes and these tech systems being overwhelmed. We’re going for volume there.”
   Still, some Big Tech companies are making inroads with local governments. Salesforce is expected to provide the engine behind  Nevada’s forthcoming website. According to Maha Neouchy, a Salesforce spokesperson, the company is providing tech services to “more than 50 federal, state, local and private health organizations” for the purposes of vaccine management. That also includes Lake County, Illinois, among others. She declined to comment on costs. Microsoft has also signed contracts with  Oklahoma, Montana and Wisconsin to help provide the states’ online vaccination scheduling portals.
  Local governments also seem willing to pay up for these programs. Public records requests revealed that  Oklahoma is paying Microsoft nearly $500,000 to run its system from last December through the end of July. That represents nearly 1 percent of the Oklahoma State Department of Health’s $59 million information technology budget.
 Similarly, other public records from Lake County, north of Chicago, show that Carahsoft, a Salesforce partner company, told the county it would cost  $5.5 million to deliver a “Vaccine Management Solution,” known as  AllVax, for work that ran from late October through the end of 2020. The county took over operations for this digital system as of early 2021.
 Some local jurisdictions seem pleased with how Big Tech companies have helped them welcome an arguably daunting number of visitors to their sites.
   “Overall I’ve been really proud of the system and that we haven't had an outage yet — we've had 380,000 people registered in the system,” said Jefferson McMillan-Wilhoit, director of health informatics and technology for the Lake County Health Department. He noted that without the $5.5 million deal with Salesforce, which constitutes a notable portion of his department’s $80 million annual budget, it would have been nearly impossible to set up this digital infrastructure so quickly.
 North Carolina recently signed a nearly $1.1 million contract with Google to provide services for the next year as a way to create a unified statewide system, rather than having citizens and county public health entities run their own.
 “We stood it up and it went live and we pressure-tested it for 1.4 million users [in a four-hour window] and we are getting hundreds of thousands [of hits in that same window of time],” said Sam Gibbs, North Carolina’s deputy secretary for technology and operations, noting that the state’s population is about 10.5 million.
   But public health officials note that regardless of how well tech companies create vaccination sign-up websites, there are some crucial populations they will never reach. That’s because there are still millions of Americans who need vaccines but who don’t have access to the internet to even sign up. Rhoads suggests that local health departments find ways to reach out to the populations who do not have the skills, internet access or trust in Big Tech companies to use these sites.
  “Bigger is not better. And it just goes back to volume versus equity,” said Rhoads. “If we go big, we’re not going to get equity. With that in mind if we go forward with volume as our priority, we should not be surprised in the end when we don’t get equity.”
 Cyrus Farivar is a reporter on the tech investigations unit of NBC News in San Francisco.
 April Glaser is a reporter on the tech investigations team for NBC News in San Francisco.
title: 由于整合县系统方面的困难，Google，Salesforce和Microsoft在建立全州范围的疫苗注册网站方面取得了不同的结果
title_en: Google, Salesforce, and Microsoft are having mixed results in building statewide vaccination sign-up websites, due to difficulties in integrating county systems

note:
大约一个月前，Marcin Brukiewicz赢得了Clubhouse的邀请，并开始在音频应用程序上花费数小时。波兰医师Brukiewicz参加了讨论健康护理和医疗技术未来的会议室，并在医疗创新者俱乐部和全球健康俱乐部等领域与来自世界各地的人们会面。 Brukiewicz有时会讲话，但他大多会听。他说：“我是一个内向的人，所以我不想大声说出来。”他也不讲英语，有时会发现语言障碍令人生畏。然后，一个朋友抱怨说，主持人很难知道在大范围的观众中谁该打来电话，而Brukiewicz有一个主意：如果有一个Clubhouse功能可以输入您的想法，该怎么办？
 上周末，布鲁基维奇（Brukiewicz）建立了Ask Clubhouse，这是一个简单的网络工具，用于管理Clubhouse上的观众问答。主持人可以设置“面板”，将其链接到俱乐部会议室，并邀请听众键入他们的问题或评论，然后mod可以实时查看这些问题或评论。 Brukiewicz在周末推出了该网站。他说，周一，人们创建了大约50个不同的委员会。他认为某些主持人将使用它来管理大量受众。其他人可能会觉得打字而不是说话感到舒适，这也可以使平台更易于访问。 Brukiewicz希望Clubhouse有一天在本地构建此功能的版本。但是，现在，人们可以使用他的解决方法。
 Clubhouse于去年春季推出时，为早期采用者提供了一个亲密的聚会场所。但是在过去的两个月中，仅邀请应用程序已迅速普及，据研究人员Vajresh Balaji称，该应用程序现已拥有超过1000万用户。这些新用户中的许多人都对如何使平台变得更好或如何利用现有功能最大化功能提出了想法。 Brukiewicz的工具只是上个月发明的众多工具之一：Clubpad提供了可在会所房间使用的免费音板：鼓声，悲伤的长号，来自Jeopardy！的计时器，DJ喇叭。 Host Notes为主持人提供了链接，会议议程和讨论摘要的空间。 Clubhouse Recorder是一种Telegram机器人，可让人们录制房间的音频。 ClubLink，Clubhype和Short.club都缩短了与Clubhouse房间的链接，使它们更易于在社交媒体上共享。至少有四个应用程序可在Clubhouse头像周围添加彩色戒指。
  其他用户则关注Clubhouse惊人的10亿美元估值，他们找到了一种赚钱的方法。还有另一种音板-该音板在App Store中以2.99美元的价格售出。 Direcon为按月付费的高级用户提供分析功能；它已经筹集了种子轮。产品策略师和Clubhouse的早期用户Chris Messina表示：“如今，这个平房行业的人们正在试图利用这一平台来利用这一机会。”
  梅西纳（Messina）也是Instagram和Twitter的早期采用者，他说，一旦平台达到一定的成熟水平，这种共生就会发生。一些用户最终构建了使自己的体验更好的功能：例如，Imgur最初是在Reddit上共享照片的一种变通方法，距平台引入本机图像上传功能还不到七年。墨西拿本人以在Twitter上创建主题标签而闻名，这是一种将平台组织成想法组的方式。 Twitter后来接受了这个想法并予以支持。他说：“人们可以解决自己的问题。”
  在Clubhouse上，没有什么不同。人们一直在应用程序的限制内工作，以使其适合他们。例如：无法共享图像，因此某些人使用其头像来显示可能与房间相关的图表或其他视觉效果。无法共享注释或链接，因此有些人将链接树添加到自己的个人资料中，从而可以找到更多信息。 Clubhouse没有UI来显示正在记录的房间，因此人们在表情符号中向正在记录的房间名称添加红点。
  有些人采取了这种创造性的解决问题的方法，进一步发展了自己的业务。被称为“俱乐部会所的Eventbrite”的YoYo俱乐部为主持人提供了一种计划和推广未来俱乐部会所客房的方式，从而消除了俱乐部会所的通知声。 YoYo Club的联合创始人彼得·斯温（Peter Swain）说：“跟随人们，然后您每小时就会收到60条Clubhouse通知。” “因此，我实际上不再从我感兴趣的人那里收到通知。同时，人们走进房间说：“我希望几个小时前就知道这一点。”” Swain和他的联合创始人认识到了这种需求，并在几周内为该应用程序制作了原型。现在，主持人可以使用它向预定的即将举行的活动的观众显示。 
尽管Swain希望高级用户最终愿意为此付费，但YoYo Club目前可以免费使用。正如他所看到的那样，平台上已经有数以百万计的追随者，并且有一群新兴的有影响力的人每周主持数十个房间。作为专职Clubhouse的托管人，这些人可能需要高级功能来增强业务。他还知道，Clubhouse仍处于起步阶段，可以通过在本地构建它来轻松实现其目标。他说：“如果您想基于他人平台建立业务模型，那将是一种风险。” “会所会建造我所建造的吗？大概。我可以领先他们一步吗？也可能。”
 Direcon，即所谓的“ Tweetdeck for Clubhouse”，已经通过向用户收取每月50美元的订阅费来利用其工具套件获利。它可以衡量一个房间中听众的总数，平均收听时间，举手的数量以及平均“粘性”。房间的。 Direcon最初是供Instagram用户使用的工具，但在一个已经饱和的市场中发现了自己。本月，由五位土耳其企业家组成的联合创始人重新发明了Clubhouse的应用程序。
 “对Clubhouse的缺乏见识使我们很难理解竞选活动的表现，我们认为应该尽快进行开发，” Direcon的联合创始人BuğraKaan Ayaz说。他说，已经有数千名主持人开始使用该服务。目标是成为“ Clubhouse的最先进的分析平台”。这可能意味着稳定的订阅收入来源，或者意味着收购：回想一下Tweetdeck在2011年被Twitter收购，据报道为4000万美元。
 对于潜在的会所企业家来说，这并非一帆风顺。该公司没有官方的API或应用程序编程接口，该接口不允许外部开发人员“插入”平台。否则，Direcon等第三方应用程序将依靠变通办法来实现其功能。 2月22日首次启动时，Direcon要求用户使用其Clubhouse用户名和密码登录，以便访问会议室数据并提供分析。在人们指出这种方法带来了安全性问题以及完全禁止用户使用Clubhouse的风险之后，Ayaz说该应用程序已更改了标识，不再要求人们使用其Clubhouse凭据登录。他补充说：“与分析相关的所有实时功能将保持不变。” “我们的传入审核功能将略有不同。”在对Direcon的博客文章和Twitter的评论中，一些用户声称他们在使用Clubhouse帐户时遇到了麻烦。阿亚兹说，这是由于Clubhouse的错误，而不是Direcon的错误。该公司的另一则帖子在一夜之间得到了更新，表示该问题已解决。
  “ Clubhouse现在必须做出一项行政决定：我们是否要允许其他人来构建应用程序？”墨西拿说。其他社交媒体公司（例如Facebook和Twitter）已使用其API来鼓励整个开发人员生态系统，从而使他们的产品对更多人更加有用，从而实现了更大的增长。但是，第三方应用程序也可能存在风险。对于像Clubhouse这样的年轻公司来说，这意味着放弃对尚未完全建立的平台和业务模型的控制。尽管API可以使用户无需交出密码即可尝试第三方应用，但历史表明，这并不总是意味着用户会更安全。
 Twitter和Instagram等其他社交平台也面临这些问题。墨西拿说，唯一的区别是时间。 “会所已经不到一年了，我们已经看到Twitter花费了五到六年的时间。”
 俱乐部会所未回复置评请求。该平台仍处于内测阶段，并且对其计划一直保持警惕。不过，如果您仍在等待俱乐部会所邀请，也将有一个新兴的获利者生态系统来帮助您。

note_en:
About a month ago, Marcin Brukiewicz scored an invite to Clubhouse and started spending hours on the audio app. Brukiewicz, a Polish physician, joined rooms discussing the future of health care and medical technology, and met people from all around the world in spaces like the Healthcare Innovators Club and the Global Health Club. Brukiewicz sometimes talks, but he mostly listens. “I’m an introvert, so I don’t like to speak up,” he says. He’s also not a native English speaker, and sometimes finds the language barrier intimidating. Then a friend complained that it’s difficult for moderators to know who to call on in a large audience, and Brukiewicz had an idea: What if there were a Clubhouse feature where you could type your thoughts?
 Over the weekend, Brukiewicz built  Ask Clubhouse, a simple web tool for managing audience Q&As on Clubhouse. Moderators can set up a “board,” link it to a Clubhouse room, and invite audience members to type their questions or comments, which the mods can then see in real time. Brukiewicz launched the site over the weekend; on Monday, he said, people had created about 50 different boards. He thinks some moderators will use it to manage large audiences; other people might find comfort in typing rather than talking, which could also make the platform more accessible. Brukiewicz hopes Clubhouse one day builds a version of this feature natively. For now, though, people can use his workaround.
 When  Clubhouse launched last spring, it served as an intimate gathering space for the early adopter crowd. But in the past two months, the invite-only app has exploded in popularity, and now has over 10 million users according to the researcher  Vajresh Balaji. Many of those new users are arriving on the platform with ideas about how to make it better, or how to maximize functionality with existing features. Brukiewicz’s tool is just one of many that have been invented in the last month:  Clubpad offers a free soundboard for use in Clubhouse rooms: a drumroll, a sad trombone, the timer from  Jeopardy!, a DJ horn.  Host Notes gives moderators a space for links, meeting agendas, and discussion summaries.  Clubhouse Recorder is a Telegram bot lets people record audio from a room.  ClubLink,  Clubhype, and  shorten.club each shorten links to Clubhouse rooms to make them easier to share on social media. There  are  at  least  four apps for adding a colorful ring around your Clubhouse avatar.
  Other users, eyeing Clubhouse’s staggering $1 billion valuation, see a way to make money. There’s  another soundboard—this one sold for $2.99 in the App Store.  Direcon offers analytics for power users who pay a monthly fee; it’s already raised a seed round. “There’s this cottage industry of people now getting on the platform who are trying to capitalize on the moment,” says Chris Messina, a product strategist and early Clubhouse user.
  Messina, who was also an early adopter on Instagram and Twitter, says this kind of symbiosis happens once platforms reach a certain level of maturation. Some users end up building functionality that make their own experience better: Imgur, for example, started as a workaround for sharing photos on Reddit, seven years before the platform introduced a native image-upload feature. Messina himself is well known for  creating the hashtag on Twitter as a way to organize the platform into groups of ideas; Twitter later took the idea and ran with it. “People solve their own problems,” he says.
  On Clubhouse, it’s no different. People have been working within the constraints of the app to make it work for them. For example: There’s no way to share images, so some people use their avatar to show charts or other visuals that may be relevant to a room. There’s no way to share notes or links, so some people add link trees to their bio where more information can be found. Clubhouse does not have the UI to show which rooms are on-the-record, so people add red dots, in emoji, to the names of rooms that are being recorded.
  Some have taken that kind of creative problem-solving further, to build their own businesses.  YoYo Club—advertised as the “Eventbrite for Clubhouse”—gives moderators a way to plan and promote future Clubhouse rooms, cutting through the noise of Clubhouse’s notifications. “You follow people and then you end up getting 60 Clubhouse notifications every hour,” says Peter Swain, YoYo Club’s cofounder. “So I’m not actually getting notifications from the people I’m interested in anymore. And meanwhile, people come into rooms saying, ‘I wish I’d known about this a few hours ago.’” Recognizing the need, Swain and his cofounder prototyped the app in a few weeks. Right now, moderators can use it to show audience members when they have upcoming events scheduled.
 YoYo Club is currently free to use, though Swain hopes that power users will eventually want to pay for it. As he sees it, there are already people with millions of followers on the platform, and an emerging set of influencers who host dozens of rooms a week. Those people will likely need premium features to enhance  their businesses, as full-time Clubhouse hosts. He’s also aware that Clubhouse is still in its early days and could easily put the kibosh on his idea by building it natively. “If you want to base your business model around somebody else’s platform, that’s a risk,” he says. “Will Clubhouse build what I built? Probably. Can I stay one step ahead of them? Also probably.”
 Direcon, the so-called “Tweetdeck for Clubhouse,” is already monetizing its suite of tools, by charging users a $50 monthly subscription fee. It can measure the total number of listeners in a room, average time listened, number of hands raised, and the average "stickiness" of the room. Direcon began as a tool for Instagram users, but found itself in an already-saturated market. This month, its cofounders—a group of five Turkish entrepreneurs—reinvented the app for Clubhouse.
 “The lack of insights on Clubhouse makes it really hard to understand the campaigns’ performance and we thought it should be developed as soon as possible,” says Buğra Kaan Ayaz, one Direcon’s cofounders. Already, he says that a few thousand moderators have started using the service. The goal is to become “the most advanced analytics platform for Clubhouse.” That could either mean a steady stream of subscription income, or an acquisition: Recall that Tweetdeck was  purchased by Twitter in 2011, for a reported $40 million.
 It’s not all smooth sailing for would-be Clubhouse entrepreneurs. The company does not have an official API, or  application programming interface, which allows outside developers to “plug into” a platform. Without that, third-party apps like Direcon rely on workarounds for their functionality. When it first launched on February 22, Direcon asked users to log in with their Clubhouse username and password, in order to access room data and provide analytics. After people pointed out that this method presented security issues and a risk of users being banned from Clubhouse altogether, Ayaz says the app changed tack and no longer requires people to sign in with their Clubhouse credentials. “All the live features related to analytics will remain the same,” he adds. “Our incoming moderation feature will be slightly different.” In comments on Direcon’s  blog post and on Twitter, some users claimed they were having trouble with their Clubhouse accounts. Ayaz says this was due to a bug with Clubhouse, not Direcon;  another post by the company was updated overnight to say the issue had been resolved.
  “Clubhouse now has to make an executive decision: Do we want to allow other people to build apps?” says Messina. Other social media companies like Facebook and Twitter have used their APIs to encourage whole developer ecosystems that made their products more useful to even more people, enabling that much more growth. Third-party apps can also be a risk, however. For a young company like Clubhouse, it means giving up some control of a platform and business model that has yet to be fully established. And while APIs can enable users to try third-party apps without handing over their passwords,  history  has  shown that doesn’t always mean users are more secure.
 Other social platforms, like Twitter and Instagram, have also faced these questions. The only difference, Messina says, is the timing. “Clubhouse has been out for less than a year, and we’re already seeing what it took five to six years for Twitter to go through.”
 Clubhouse did not respond to requests for comment. The platform is still in closed beta, and has been fairly guarded about its plans. If you’re still waiting for your Clubhouse invite, though, there’s a  burgeoning  ecosystem of profiteers to help with that, too.

凯文·凯利（Kevin Kelly）在他的2008年经典文章“ 1000个真正的粉丝”中预测互联网将改变创意活动的经济性：
 要成为一名成功的创作者，您不需要数百万。您不需要数百万美元或数百万的客户，数百万的客户或数百万的粉丝。要以手工艺人，摄影师，音乐家，设计师，作家，动画师，应用程序制作者，企业家或发明家为生，您只需要成千上万的忠实粉丝。
 真正的粉丝是指可以购买任何产品的粉丝。这些顽固的粉丝会开车200英里才能看到你唱歌；他们会购买您的精装本，平装本和听觉版本；他们将购买您下一个看不见的雕像景象；他们将为您的免费YouTube频道的“最佳” DVD版本付费；他们将每月一次来到您的厨师的餐桌上。
 凯利（Kelly）的愿景是，互联网是终极的媒人，可以推动21世纪的惠顾。创作者，无论多么看似利基市场，现在都可以发现他们的真正粉丝，而他们又会通过直接的资金支持来展示他们的热情。
 但是互联网绕道而行。集中式社交平台成为创作者和粉丝建立联系的主要方式。这些平台利用这种能力成为新的中介机构-在创作者和用户之间插入广告和算法推荐，同时自己承担大部分收入。
 好消息是，互联网正在重新发展回凯利（Kelly）的愿景。例如，Substack上的许多顶级作家的收入远高于受薪工作。低获取率加上热情的拥护者的经济确实令人惊奇。在Substack上，有1,000个通讯订阅者每月向作者支付10美元/年，净收入超过10万美元。
 加密货币，特别是NFT（不可替代的代币），可以加速创作者直接通过其粉丝获利的趋势。社交平台将继续对建立受众群体有用（尽管也应将其替换为更好的分散式替代方案），但是创作者可以越来越多地依靠其他方法（包括NFT和启用加密货币的经济体）来赚钱。 
NFT是基于区块链的记录，唯一代表媒体。媒体可以是任何数字形式，包括艺术，视频，音乐，gif，游戏，文本，模因和代码。 NFT包含了其历史和起源的高度值得信赖的文档，并且可以附加代码来执行程序员梦dream以求的几乎任何事情（一种流行的功能是可以确保原始创作者从二次销售中获得版税的代码）。 NFT由相同的技术保护，该技术使比特币可以被全世界数亿人拥有，并代表着数千亿美元的价值。
 由于销量高，NFT最近受到了很多关注。在过去30天里，NFT的销售额已超过3亿美元：
  加密货币有兴衰周期的历史，很可能NFT会有自己的起伏。
 也就是说，NFT为创作者提供从根本上更好的经济效益的三个重要原因。首先，已经提到了这一点，就是取消寻租中介。区块链的逻辑是，一旦您购买了NFT，就可以完全控制它，就像在现实世界中购买书籍或运动鞋一样。 NFT平台和市场已经存在并且将继续存在，但是它们将受到其收费的约束，因为基于区块链的所有权将权力转移给了创作者和用户-您可以货比三家并强迫市场赚取其费用。 （请注意，降低中介费可能会对创建者的可支配收入产生乘数效应。例如，如果您赚取了10万美元的收入并有8万美元的成本，则削减50％的可乘性，将可支配收入的6倍从20000美元乘以$ 120K。）
 NFT改变创作者经济的第二种方法是启用细粒度的价格分层。在基于广告的模型中，无论粉丝的热情程度如何，收入都是或多或少统一产生的。与Substack一样，NFT通过向创作者提供价格更高的特殊项目，使创作者可以“激读”最热情的用户。但是NFT比非加密产品走得更远，因为它们很容易切成薄片并切成降序的一系列定价层。 NBA Top Shot卡的价格从10万美元到几美元不等。比特币的粉丝？您可以购买任意数量的商品，最多购买8个小数点，具体取决于您的热情程度。 Crypto的细粒度使创建者可以在需求曲线下捕获更大的区域。
  NFT改变创造者经济的第三个也是最重要的方法是让用户成为所有者，从而将客户获取成本降低到接近零。打开任何Tech S-1档案，您将看到庞大的用户/客户获取成本，通常是在线广告或销售人员的支出。相比之下，加密货币的总市值已增长至一万亿美元以上，几乎没有营销支出。比特币和以太坊没有背后的组织，更不用说营销预算了，而是被成千上万的人使用，拥有和喜爱。
 迄今为止，收入最高的NFT项目NBA Top Shot在过去的一个月中创造了2亿美元的销售总额，而在营销上的支出却很少。它之所以能够如此高效地发展，是因为用户感觉像所有者一样-他们在游戏中占了上风。在社区，兴奋和所有权的推动下，这是真正的点对点营销。 
NFT仍处于早期阶段，并且将会发展。随着围绕它们的数字体验的建立，它们的效用将会提高，包括市场，社交网络，展示，游戏和虚拟世界。与NFT配对的其他面向消费者的加密产品也可能会出现。像Fortnite这样的现代视频游戏包含复杂的经济体，这些经济体将V-Bucks等可替代代币与NFT /皮肤等虚拟商品混合在一起。有一天，每个互联网社区都可能拥有自己的微观经济，包括NFT和用户可以使用，拥有和收集的可替代令牌。
 成千上万的忠实拥护者论点建立在互联网的原始理想之上：全球连接的用户和创作者，不受中介机构的束缚，共享思想和经济上的发展。现有的社交媒体平台通过将创作者锁定在分发和货币化捆绑中而偏离了这一愿景。相应地，有两种方法可以挑战他们：吸引用户或赚钱。加密货币和NFT为我们提供了一种获取现金的新方式。一起让它成为现实。
   此处表达的观点是各个AH Capital Management，L.L.C.的观点。 （“ a16z”）人员被引用，并非a16z或其关联公司的观点。这里包含的某些信息是从第三方渠道获得的，包括从a16z管理的基金的投资组合公司。尽管a16z从可靠的来源中获取，但并未独立验证此类信息，也未表示该信息的持久准确性或其在特定情况下的适用性。此外，此内容可能包括第三方广告； a16z尚未审查此类广告，也不认可其中包含的任何广告内容。
 提供此内容仅供参考，不应作为法律，业务，投资或税务建议。您应该就这些问题咨询自己的顾问。对任何证券或数字资产的引用仅出于说明目的，并不构成投资建议或提供投资咨询服务的要约。此外，此内容既不针对也不打算供任何投资者或准投资者使用，并且在任何情况下，在决定对a16z管理的任何基金进行投资时都不得依赖。 （仅可通过私募备忘录，认购协议以及任何此类基金的其他相关文件提出对a16z基金的投资提议，并应完整阅读。）提及，提及或提及的任何投资或投资组合公司所描述的内容并不代表a16z管理的所有车辆投资，也无法保证这些投资将获利或将来进行的其他投资具有类似的特征或结果。 https://a16z.com/investments上提供了由安德森·霍洛维茨（Andreessen Horowitz）管理的基金进行的投资清单（不包括发行人未获a16z许可公开披露的投资以及未公开交易的公开交易数字资产的投资）。 /。

note_en:
In his classic 2008 essay “  1000 True Fans  , ”  Kevin Kelly predicted that the internet would transform the economics of creative activities:
 To be a successful creator you don’t need millions. You don’t need millions of dollars or millions of customers, millions of clients or millions of fans. To make a living as a craftsperson, photographer, musician, designer, author, animator, app maker, entrepreneur, or inventor you need only thousands of true fans.
 A true fan is defined as a fan that will buy anything you produce. These diehard fans will drive 200 miles to see you sing; they will buy the hardback and paperback and audible versions of your book; they will purchase your next figurine sight unseen; they will pay for the “best-of” DVD version of your free YouTube channel; they will come to your chef’s table once a month.
 Kelly’s vision was that the internet was the ultimate matchmaker, enabling 21st century patronage. Creators, no matter how seemingly niche, could now discover their true fans, who would in turn demonstrate their enthusiasm through direct financial support.
 But the internet took a detour. Centralized social platforms became the dominant way for creators and fans to connect. The platforms used this power to become the new intermediaries — inserting ads and algorithmic recommendations between creators and users while keeping most of the revenue for themselves.
 The good news is that the internet is trending back to Kelly’s vision. For example, many top writers on Substack earn far more than they did at salaried jobs. The economics of low take rates plus enthusiastic fandom does wonders. On Substack, 1,000 newsletter subscribers paying $10/month nets over $100K/year to the writer.
 Crypto, and specifically   NFTs  (non-fungible tokens), can accelerate the trend of creators monetizing directly with their fans. Social platforms will continue to be useful for building audiences (although these too should probably be replaced with superior   decentralized  alternatives), but creators can increasingly rely on other methods including NFTs and crypto-enabled economies to make money.
 NFTs are blockchain-based records that uniquely represent pieces of media. The media can be anything digital, including art, videos, music, gifs, games, text, memes, and code. NFTs contain highly trustworthy documentation of their history and origin, and can have code attached to do almost anything programmers dream up (one popular feature is code that ensures that the original creator receives royalties from secondary sales). NFTs are secured by the same technology that enabled Bitcoin to be owned by hundreds of millions of people around the world and represent hundreds of billions of dollars of value.
 NFTs have received a lot of attention lately because of high sales volumes. In the past 30 days there has been over   $300M  in NFT sales:
  Crypto has a history of boom and bust cycles, and it’s very possible NFTs will have their own ups and downs.
 That said, there are three important reasons why NFTs offer fundamentally better economics for creators. The first, already alluded to above, is by removing rent-seeking intermediaries. The logic of blockchains is once you purchase an NFT it is yours to fully control, just like when you buy books or sneakers in the real world. There are and will continue to be NFT platforms and marketplaces, but they will be constrained in what they can charge because blockchain-based ownership shifts the power back to creators and users — you can shop around and force the marketplace to earn its fees. (Note that lowering the intermediary fees can have a multiplier effect on creator disposable income. For example, if you make $100K in revenue and have $80K in costs, cutting out a 50% take rate multiples your disposable income 6x from $20K to $120K.)
 The second way NFTs change creator economics is by enabling granular price tiering. In ad-based models, revenue is generated more or less uniformly regardless of the fan’s enthusiasm level. As with Substack, NFTs allow the creator to “cream skim” the most passionate users by offering them special items which cost more. But NFTs go farther than non-crypto products in that they are easily sliced and diced into a descending series of pricing tiers. NBA Top Shot cards range from over $100K to a few dollars. Fan of Bitcoin? You can buy as much or little as you want, down to 8 decimal points, depending on your level of enthusiasm. Crypto’s fine-grained granularity lets creators capture a much larger area under the demand curve.
  The third and most important way NFTs change creator economics is by making users owners, thereby reducing customer acquisition costs to near zero. Open any tech   S-1  filing and you’ll see massive user/customer acquisition costs, usually going to online ads or sales staff. Crypto, by contrast, has grown to over a trillion dollars in aggregate market capitalization with almost no marketing spend. Bitcoin and Ethereum don’t have organizations behind them let alone marketing budgets, yet are used, owned, and loved by tens of millions of people.
 The highest revenue NFT project to date,   NBA Top Shot , has generated $200M in gross sales in just the past month while spending very little on marketing. It’s been able to grow so efficiently because users feel like owners — they have skin in the game. It’s true peer-to-peer marketing, fueled by community,   excitement , and ownership.
 NFTs are still early, and will evolve. Their utility will increase as digital experiences are built around them, including marketplaces, social networks, showcases, games, and virtual worlds. It’s also likely that other consumer-facing crypto products emerge that pair with NFTs. Modern video games like Fortnite contain sophisticated economies that mix fungible tokens like V-Bucks with NFTs/virtual goods like skins. Someday every internet community might have its own micro-economy, including NFTs and fungible tokens that users can use, own, and collect.
 The thousand true fans thesis builds on the original ideals of the internet: users and creators globally connected, unconstrained by intermediaries, sharing ideas and economic upside. Incumbent social media platforms sidetracked this vision by locking creators into a bundle of distribution and monetization. There are, correspondingly, two ways to challenge them: take the users, or take the money. Crypto and NFTs give us a new way to take the money. Let’s make it happen.

新型M1 MacBook令人印象深刻的性能和电池续航能力的提高在通常平静的转售市场上造成了历史性的中断。您是否应该花800美元购买一台使用一年的MacBook Air，而再花200美元就能获得性能提高几倍，电池寿命延长50％的MacBook Air？
 精明的买家在问自己一个问题。毫不奇怪，最常见的答案似乎是“不！”
       我会定期检查Craigslist，以跟踪待售商品。我发现MacBook的定价出现了不同寻常的分歧。
 还有更多的稍后型号的英特尔MacBook即将发售。其中一些显示了上下文相关的价格，即几乎新的MacBook Air售价为600美元，而不是一些认为他们基于Intel的机器仍然值得的800-900美元。
   除非买家检查出像Everymac这样的网站，否则他们将不知道自己所缺少的东西。最底层的M1 MacBook Air的Geekbench 5多处理器得分几乎是2020年初的顶级四核I7的2.5倍。价格的80％。而且，由于内存管理和页面交换非常高效，因此大多数用户无需为16GB版本花费额外的现金。
   在比较MacBook Pro时，对比度更加惊人。 13＆＃34;在Geekbench 5单处理器和多处理器基准测试中，MacBook Pro的速度比顶级16＆＃34;更快。 MacBook Pro英特尔I9，价格不到一半。
 这不仅仅是一个基准。在M1 MacBook Pro和16 MacBook Pro上进行搜索。在YouTube上观看多部视频，测试两台计算机上的实际工作负载。 
公平地说，并不是所有人都对它印象深刻，这通常是因为对他们的工作流程至关重要的软件并未针对新的M1处理器进行优化。但是这些极端情况并不能反映普通用户的需求。同时，大多数专业应用程序将在明年被重新编译为M1本机。
  不幸的是，英特尔在每代新一代x86处理器上获得惊人性能提升的日子比我们落后了几十年。自从Intel 8008跃升至8080以来，业界一直没有看到这种价格/性能不连续的水平。
 好消息：假设苹果为零售商提供价格保护，那么英特尔书籍方面的最佳交易尚未到来。似乎有大量基于Intel的MacBook库存，它们必须在2021年底之前清除它们。起飞时，Costco所提供的降价幅度甚至超过250美元。
 严重的二手Mac大屠杀仍然遥遥领先。当您的20,000美元的Mac Pro被8,000美元的Mx Mac Pro击败时-我在这里guess测呢？
 如果您拥有Intel MacBook Air或MacBook Pro，并且正在考虑进行交易，则可以通过尽快而不是稍后的方式从当前的书中获得更多。苹果硅的故事只会变得更好。而且旧款Mac的转售价值只会更差。
 欢迎发表评论。我将很快发布我的M1 MacBook Air中期评测。并不是所有的彩虹和独角兽，但乍一看也不错。
note_en:
The impressive performance and battery life gains of the  new M1 MacBooks have created a historic discontinuity in the normally placid resale market. Should you spend $800 for a one year old MacBook Air when for $200 more you could get a MacBook Air with several times the performance and 50 percent better battery life?
 That's a question savvy buyers are asking themselves. Not surprisingly, the most common answer seems to be "Nope!"
       I check Craigslist fairly regularly to keep track of what's for sale. I've seen an unusual bifurcation in the pricing for MacBooks.
 There are more late-model Intel MacBooks showing up for sale. Some of those are showing context sensitive pricing, i.e. almost new MacBook Airs for $600 rather than the $800-$900 that some think their Intel-based machine is still worth.
   Unless buyers check out a site like  Everymac they won't know what they're missing. The bottom-of-the-line M1 MacBook Air has a Geekbench 5 multiprocessor score that is almost 2.5x that of the early 2020, top-of-the-line quad-core I7. For 80 percent of the price. And most users won't need to spend the extra cash for the 16GB version since the memory management and page swapping is so efficient.
   The contrast is even more striking when comparing MacBook Pros. Not only is the 13" MacBook Pro faster on the Geekbench 5 single and multiprocessor benchmarks than the top-of-the-line 16" MacBook Pro Intel I9, it less than half the price.
 And it isn't just a single benchmark. Search on "M1 MacBook Pro vs 16 MacBook Pro" on YouTube to see multiple videos testing real world workloads on both machines.
 To be fair, not everyone is impressed, often because the software critical to their workflow isn't optimized for the new M1 processor. But those corner cases don't reflect average users needs. In the meantime, most of those specialized apps are being recompiled to be M1 native over the next year.
  The days when Intel produced startling performance gains with every new generation of x86 processors are, sadly, decades behind us. The industry hasn't seen this level of price/performance discontinuity since the jump from the Intel 8008 to the 8080.
 The good news: the best deals on Intel 'Books are yet to come, assuming Apple offers retailers price protection. There seems to be a large inventory of Intel based MacBooks and they have to clear them out before the end of 2021. Taking off even more than the $250 cut that Costco, for one, is offering.
 The serious used Mac carnage is still ahead. What happens when your $20,000 Mac Pro gets beat by  -- and I'm guesstimating here -- an $8,000 Mx Mac Pro?
 If you have an Intel MacBook Air or MacBook Pro and are thinking of trading up, you're likely to get more for your current 'Book by moving sooner rather than later. The Apple Silicon story is only going to get better. And the resale value of older Macs only worse.
 Comments welcome. I'll be publishing my medium-term M1 MacBook Air review soon. It's not all rainbows and unicorns, but not bad for a first effort.
title: 2021年Mac价格暴跌

note:
考古学家从庞贝城外的一座别墅中发现了一种独特的罗马礼仪车厢，庞贝城是在公元79年被火山喷发掩埋的。
 几乎保存完好的四轮马车，由铁，青铜和锡制成，被发现在奇维塔朱利亚纳一栋古别墅的马near附近，该别墅位于庞贝古城墙以北约700米处，靠近三匹马的遗骸所在。于2018年出土，包括仍在使用中的一具。
  庞贝考古遗址即将离任的负责人马西莫·奥萨纳（Massimo Osanna）表示，马车是该地区发现的第一款马车，到目前为止，马车已经生产出用于运输和工作的实用车辆，但没有举行仪式。
 Osanna说：“这是一个非凡的发现，增进了我们对古代世界的了解。”他补充说，马车将伴随着社区的喜庆时刻，例如游行和游行。
  在那不勒斯东南14英里处的庞贝城，由于它承受着相当于许多原子弹的爆发力，因此被埋在火山灰，浮石和尘土中时，大约有13,000人居住。
  在这个66公顷（165英亩）的古镇中，约有三分之二被发现。直到16世纪才发现废墟，大约1750年开始进行有组织的发掘。
 根据公园的一份声明，在奇维塔朱利亚纳（Civita Giuliana）附近的发掘活动是该地区旨在打击非法活动的计划的一部分，该活动包括挖掘隧道以达到可以在非法市场上出售的文物。 
公园的声明说，抢劫者错过了战车放置了将近2000年的房间，双方都在挖洞。  专家补充说，专家们非常谨慎地挖掘车辆，例如将石膏倒入空隙中，以保留已分解的任何有机材料的烙印。  公园说，这使它得以保存完好，直到绳索的烙印，“从而揭示了战车的所有复杂性”。  意大利文化大臣达里奥·弗朗切斯基尼（Dario Franceschini）表示：“庞贝古城的发现不断使我们赞叹不已，并且将持续很多年，还有20公顷土地尚待挖掘。”  庞贝古城是希腊罗马人生活中难得的快照，是意大利最受欢迎的景点之一，也是联合国教科文组织世界遗产。
note_en:
Archaeologists have unearthed a unique Roman ceremonial carriage from a villa just outside Pompeii, the city buried in a volcanic eruption in 79 AD.
 The almost perfectly preserved four-wheeled carriage, made of iron, bronze and tin, was found near the stables of an ancient villa at Civita Giuliana, about 700 metres north of the walls of ancient Pompeii and close to where the remains of three horses were unearthed in 2018, including one still in its harness.
  Massimo Osanna, the outgoing director of the Pompeii archaeological site, said the carriage was the first of its kind discovered in the area, which had so far yielded functional vehicles used for transport and work, but not for ceremonies.
 “This is an extraordinary discovery that advances our understanding of the ancient world,” Osanna said, adding that the carriage would have accompanied festive moments for the community, such as parades and processions.
  Pompeii, 14 miles south-east of Naples, was home to about 13,000 people when it was buried under ash, pumice pebbles and dust as it endured the force of an eruption equivalent to many atomic bombs.
  About two-thirds of the 66-hectare (165-acre) ancient town has been uncovered. The ruins were not discovered until the 16th century and organised excavations began in about 1750.
 According to a statement from the park, the excavation around Civita Giuliana is part of a programme aimed at fighting illegal activity in the area, including tunnel digging to reach artefacts that can be sold on illicit markets.
 Looters missed the room where the chariot had lain for almost 2,000 years, tunnelling by on both sides, the park’s statement said.
 Specialists took great care to unearth the vehicle, for example by pouring plaster into voids to preserve the imprint of any organic material that had decomposed, it added.
 The park said this had allowed it to emerge well preserved down to the imprints of ropes, “thus revealing the chariot in all of its complexity”.
 “Pompeii continues to amaze us with its discoveries and it will do so for many years, with 20 hectares still to be dug up,” said Italy’s culture minister, Dario Franceschini.
 A rare snapshot of Greco-Roman life, Pompeii is one of Italy’s most popular attractions and a Unesco world heritage site.

跳转至导航跳转至搜索加密无政府状态（或加密无政府状态）是一种政治意识形态，侧重于保护隐私，政治自由和经济自由，其拥护者使用加密软件来实现机密性和安全性，同时通过计算机网络发送和接收信息。 。 [1]
 通过使用加密软件，除非用户显示关联，否则很难找到特定用户或组织的身份与他们使用的假名之间的关联。很难说哪个国家的法律将被忽略[需要澄清]，因为甚至某个参与者的位置也是未知的。但是，从理论上讲，参与者可以使用智能合约自愿制定新法律，或者，如果用户是笔名，则取决于在线声誉。 [原始研究？]
   在他的1988年＆＃34;密码无政府主义者宣言（Timothy C. May）介绍了密码无政府主义的基本原理，确保完全匿名，完全言论自由和完全贸易自由的加密交易所-来自国家的可预见敌意。 [2]
  ＆＃34; Crypto-＆＃34;来自古希腊语κρυπτόςkruptós，意思是“隐藏”。或“秘密”。密码无政府主义是指建立在密码方法基础上的无政府主义政治，以及一种秘密运行的无政府主义形式。 [3]
  密码无政府主义者的动机之一是防御对计算机网络通信的监视。密码无政府主义者试图防止政府大规模监视，例如PRISM，Tempora，电信数据保留，NSA无根据监视争议，641A室，FRA等。密码无政府主义者认为加密技术的发展和使用是防范此类问题的主要手段。
 第二个问题是基于言论自由逃避审查制度，特别是互联网审查制度。密码无政府主义者使用的程序通常可以匿名地从Internet或其他计算机网络发布和读取信息。例如，Tor，I2P，Freenet和许多类似的网络都允许匿名＆＃34; hidden＆＃34;。网页只能由这些程序的用户访问，而像Bitmessage这样的项目则允许使用匿名消息传递系统来替代电子邮件。这有助于压迫性国家的举报人和政治反对派传播他们的信息。
 第三个原因是建立并参与反经济学，其中包括开发银行系统可行的替代方案，以及开发替代金融系统，从而为用户提供更大的隐私权或匿名性的选择。丝绸之路和黑市重载等服务使交易商品和服务成为可能，而不受法律的干扰很小。这些是集中化的市场或工具或工具的示例。同样，一些比特币用户使用的网络钱包也很集中且容易受到攻击。分散和分散的市场和货币交易所更难被执法机构确定目标，并可能为其最终用户提供更多的安全性。 OpenBazaar或Particl是开发中分散和分散市场的一个示例。 
开发和维护这些密码系统的技术挑战是巨大的，这使一些程序员对加入这样的项目感兴趣。
  密码无政府主义者认为，如果没有加密功能，则消息，个人信息和私人生活将受到严重破坏。他们争辩说，禁止使用密码学等同于消除通信的机密性。他们认为，只有严厉的警察国家才会将加密技术定为犯罪。在某些国家/地区使用它已经是非法的，而在其他国家/地区，出口法律具有限制性。英国的公民必须应要求向当局提供用于解密个人系统的密钥。没有这样做可能会导致最长两年的徒刑，而没有其他犯罪活动的证据。 [4]
 可以通过在短时间间隔内快速生成新的，无关的公钥和私钥，使用安全通道的自动密钥交换来规避这种立法的密钥交出策略。重新键入密钥后，可以删除旧密钥，从而使最终用户无法访问以前使用过的密钥，从而即使用户愿意也可以删除用户公开旧密钥的能力。支持这种快速重新加密的技术包括公钥加密，硬件PRNG，完美的前向保密性和机会主义加密。今天，世界各地的移动设备上普遍使用的许多应用程序都采用了这种加密方式。停止这种加密的唯一方法是完全禁止使用该加密，否则会增加其实际使用的障碍（无论是技术上还是法律上的）。这样的障碍给这种密码技术的用户带来了困难和风险，这将限制并可能阻止其广泛采用。通常，起诉的威胁限制了技术的使用和扩散，而不是技术本身的易用性。
 密码无政府主义是一种旨在创建和部署信息基础结构的意识形态，该信息基础结构在设计上无法满足要求破坏参与个体的威权主义的要求。信件的保密性。
  密码无政府主义严重依赖合理的可否认性来避免审查制度。密码无政府主义者通过将加密的消息发送到计算机网络中相互链接的代理来创建这种可否认性。路由信息的有效负载与消息捆绑在一起；该消息将使用每个代理和接收者的公共密钥进行加密。每个节点只能解密其自己的消息部分，并且只能获取针对其自身的信息。也就是说，它从哪个节点获得消息，以及应该将消息传递到哪个节点。仅访问该信息，对于网络中的节点来说，要知道它们携带的信息是什么，或者与谁进行通信是非常困难的。同行可以使用rendevouz洋葱[5]或类似的东西[6] [7]数字签名等来保护彼此的身份。最初发送信息的人和预定的接收者被认为无法检测，除非同伴们自己合作以揭示此信息。有关更多信息，请参见混合网络，洋葱路由和匿名P2P。
 匿名通信协议使得很难知道谁连接到任何特定服务或化名。在不强制禁止强密码学的情况下，很难阻止网络中任何潜在的犯罪活动。
 可否认的加密和匿名网络可用于避免在共享用户不敢在没有任何身份保护的情况下害怕共享的非法或敏感信息时被检测到。共享的信息可能包括反国家宣传，举报，麻醉品分发，非法色情内容，政治异议者的报告分发，匿名货币交易等。完全是加密无政府主义的政治行动。这个想法是，腐败的当局将因人民之间相互自由交流以及在没有当局干预能力的情况下进行组织的能力而受到损害。 
这些网络中存在无法追踪的，私人发行的电子货币和匿名互联网银行。过去，这仅由集中式组织来处理。 Digital Monetary Trust和Yodelbank就是两个这样的匿名银行的示例，后来它们的创建者将其下线。 Ukash是一个电子货币网络。最高500英镑/ 750欧元的现金可以在支付终端和零售店中兑换成19位的Ukash代金券。
 比特币是一种由对等网络设备生成和保护的货币，这些设备维护可在密码无政府状态下使用的系统内所有交易的公共记录。比特币背后的思想可以追溯到《加密无政府主义者宣言》。 [8]存在大量的山寨币，其中一些具有不透明的分类帐，因此对等方之间的交易是不可追踪的（为此，第一个协议称为Zerocoin协议，另请参见Monero）。一些山寨币也充当分散的自治组织，或充当使此类组织成为可能的平台。
 丝绸之路是第一个匿名加密货币市场。它使用Tor网络进行操作，所有交易均使用比特币。它于2013年被联邦调查局关闭。Silkroad迅速被其他加密市场所取代，如今有几个并行竞争的市场。
 OpenBazaar是一个开源项目，为完全分散的市场中的电子商务交易开发协议。 [9]它使用加密货币比特币，并受到名为DarkMarket的黑客马拉松项目的启发。
 对于可以通过Internet提供的信息服务，更容易实现匿名交易。提供实物产品更加困难，因为进入实物世界时更容易打破匿名性：供应商需要知道将实物商品发送到哪里。无法追索的金钱使人们有可能无视物质世界的某些定律，因为这些定律在不了解人们的自然身份的情况下是无法执行的。例如，如果没有政府知道服务提供商的身份，则可以避免对通过密码无政府主义者网络提供的在线服务的收入征税。
 暗杀市场是一个基于Tor的市场，由化名Kuwabatake Sanjuro的自称加密无政府主义者经营。 [10]
 蒂莫西·C（Timothy C. May）在《密码学》（The Cyphernomicon）一书中指出，加密无政府主义可以看作是无政府资本主义的一种形式： 
由此产生的结果尚不清楚，但我认为这将是一种我称之为“密码无政府状态”的无政府资本主义市场体系。 [11]
 密码学中的另一句话定义了密码无政府主义。在标题为“什么是加密无政府状态？”的情况下，May写道：
  我们中的一些人认为，各种形式的强密码技术将导致国家的权力下降，甚至可能突然崩溃。我们相信，通过安全通信，数字货币，匿名和假名以及其他加密媒介介导的互动，向网络空间的扩展将深刻改变经济和社会互动的性质。政府将很难收税，规范个人和公司的行为（至少是小公司的行为），并且通常在甚至不知道大陆人在做什么的情况下强迫人们！
    ^ Timothy C.五月（2014年12月）。 ＆＃34;加密无政府状态和虚拟社区。引用日志需要| journal =（帮助）
       ^ Chen，Adrian（2013年11月26日）。 ＆＃34;关于比特币的很多事。国际纽约时报。 （原始内容存档于2013年12月10日）。
  ^ Greenberg，Andy（2013年11月18日），＆＃34;遇见＆＃39;暗杀市场＆＃39; 2013年12月10日原始作者存档的《福布斯》杂志创作者Who's s Crowdfunding Murder with Bitcoins
    维纳·维格（Vinge）弗兰克尔·詹姆斯（Frankel，James）（2001），《真名：与网络空间前沿的开放》，Tor Books 
约翰·佩里·巴洛（Barlow，1996年2月），《网络空间独立宣言》，于2013年10月23日从原始文档存档。
